label: More than 150 communities have lost all scheduled commercial air service since 1995
source: RAA (Regional Airline Association), 'Newly Released Data Shows Air Service Crisis', November 2022, https://raa.org/newly-released-data-shows-air-service-crisis/
notes: The '150+' phrasing is confirmed in multiple RAA press releases and secondary coverage (AviationPros, AirlineGeeks, Stratus Finance). A specific '157' figure was not found in accessible documents; it may appear in the Nov 2022 PDF slide deck (raa.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/Fourth-Quarter-Small-Community-Air-Service-Deck-_Final-1.pdf) which returned 403. The figure could also reflect a later update in 2023-2024 quarterly decks.

label: 14 airports lost all scheduled commercial air service since 2019 (as of Nov 2022, 'a number still rising')
source: RAA (Regional Airline Association), 'Newly Released Data Shows Air Service Crisis', November 2022, https://raa.org/newly-released-data-shows-air-service-crisis/
notes: Confirmed across multiple secondary sources: AviationPros, AirlineGeeks (Dec 2022), Stratus Finance, The Points Guy. Later RAA Q1 2024 data cited 12 airports with all service lost (Jan 2024 vs Jan 2020), suggesting the 14 figure was specific to the Nov 2022 snapshot. Update: A 2024 RAA quarterly report noted 12 airports losing all service and 37 losing more than half.

label: Between 2019 and 2022, 161 U.S. airports lost more than one in four (25%+) of their commercial flights
source: RAA (Regional Airline Association), 'Newly Released Data Shows Air Service Crisis', November 2022, https://raa.org/newly-released-data-shows-air-service-crisis/
notes: Confirmed across multiple secondary sources: AviationPros, AirlineGeeks (Dec 2022), Airports International, Stratus Finance. RAA sourced from OAG published schedule data comparing October 2022 vs October 2019. Later 2024 RAA data cited 317 airports (74%) losing an average of 25%+ flights comparing Jan 2024 to Jan 2020.

label: The big-three network carriers (American, Delta, United) exited 68 cities since April 2020
source: Ailevon Pacific Aviation Consulting, cited in Airline Weekly/Skift 'Small Cities Lose Air Service as Large Airlines Exit', May 2023, https://airlineweekly.skift.com/2023/05/small-cities-lose-air-service-as-large-airlines-exit/
notes: The '68' figure was not directly retrieved from the Ailevon Pacific source (403 blocks). Secondary searches show the number evolved over time: 59 airports cited in a Yahoo Sports/Business Insider article (earlier 2023); 68-69 in May 2023 (Airline Weekly/Skift article); 74 in a later May 2023 LinkedIn post; 75 by early 2025. The '68' is plausible for a specific date-of-publication snapshot but the Ailevon Pacific LinkedIn update from May 15, 2023 may cite 74. Multiple sources reference Ailevon Pacific as the original data provider.

label: National count of total lost nonstop routes/city-pairs from small communities (GAO-23-105487, DOT T-100 analyses, RAA reports)
source: GAO-24-106681 'Commercial Aviation: Trends in Air Service to Small Communities' (reissued Jan 2026); GAO-26-109182 'Commercial Aviation: Air Service to Small Communities Continues to Face Challenges' (June 2026); https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-24-106681
notes: GAO-23-105487 was not found in searches; closest reports are GAO-24-106681 (2024, covering 2018-2023) and GAO-26-109182 (June 2026, covering 2018-2024). Neither publicly surfaced a specific national count of lost nonstop routes/city-pairs in accessible search summaries. GAO-24-106681 studied 218 small communities: 8% had >50% passenger decline, connectivity index declined, non-EAS nonhub airports saw 21% drop in avg daily departures per route 2018-2024 (from GAO-26-109182). No single 'X routes lost nationally' headline figure found. DOT T-100 analyses not independently surfaced. RAA quarterly decks (behind 403) likely contain route-level counts.
